title: #eng-ml: 1955006789
preview:
Priya Iyer: quick heads-up — I pushed raptor-lite-1.0-int8 to dev/staging for a first-pass quant smoke. can folks run the checklist below and flag regressions?

Mateo Ruiz: ack, will run perf/latency and kv-cache checks. ping me if you want specific benchmarking scripts.

Ava Chen: thx Priya. can we prioritize: 1) basic correctness (qa prompts), 2) tokenization edge cases, 3) streaming + kv-cache, 4) latency/ooms under batch=8?

Priya Iyer: exactly. my quick sanity prompts (run these first):
```
curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_KEY" -d '{"model":"raptor-lite-1.0-int8","input":"Summarize: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."}' https://api.redwood.dev/v1/generate
```

Priya Iyer: also do: math/regression check: "What is 37 * 24?" and instruction-following: "Translate to French: 'Edge case testing is important.'"

Jonah Park: infra note — watch for memory growth in the first 30s of multi-request bursts. last time quant-int8 path leaked a KV buffer on preempt. I'll tail the node logs.

Mateo Ruiz: ran quick run, latency looks fine (~80ms p50) but saw weird tokenization split on contractions: input "don't" produced different token ids vs fp16 variant; can share diff.

Priya Iyer: send the diff here. the checklist item: tokenization parity vs float16 and fp32 variants, and verify detokenize(roundtrip) for common punctuation/emoji.

Mateo Ruiz: attaching minimal repro (paste):
```
# repro snippet
req = {"model":"raptor-lite-1.0-int8","input":"I don't know."}
# int8 -> token ids: [101, 202, 1005, ...]
# fp16 -> token ids: [101, 2020, ...]  <-- mismatch at apostrophe
```

Priya Iyer: hmm. could be tokenizer mismatch during compilation/quant step. @ava can we gate rollout until tokenization parity is fixed?

Ava Chen: agree we shouldn't promote. prioritized: blocker if tokenization changes outputs. add canary with traffic=0.1 first and keep float16 fallback.

deploy-bot: canary created: raptor-lite-1.0-int8 -> dev-staging canary id cn-1955, traffic 0.1. logs -> /canary/cn-1955/logs

Jonah Park: I also saw 1 OOM on 16GB when batch=16 with long prompts (4096 tokens). recommend adding mem guard for quant path and failover to float16.

Priya Iyer: noted. next steps I will: 1) revert tokenizer compile flags and regen tokenizer artifact, 2) add automated sanity tests (math, translate, detokenize) to presubmit, 3) add mem-guard & fallback policy to canary. will update this thread with timestamps.

Ava Chen: thanks. small checklist for the thread to mark done: [ ] tokenization parity, [ ] basic QA prompts, [ ] math/regression tests, [ ] streaming + kv-cache stability, [ ] mem guards + fallback, [ ] perf/latency NRT. please mark when complete. :thumbsup:

Priya Iyer: marking progress here as I finish items. will post final smoke summary here when done. :hourglass:

title: #eng-oncall: 1932101234
preview:
Maya Patel: handing off now — quick summary before I go offline for the next 8h. Two active pages right now but both mitigated to degraded: 
- INC-4217 (hosted-api latency spike) — mitigation done (fallback to model-v1-hot) and autoscale bumped. Customer impact: visible latency increase for some bursty routes.
- INC-4221 (kv-cache thrash on ded-3) — temporarily pinned cache policy + restart of worker pool; throughput stabilized but we still see elevated 95p.

I'm primary for the next hour while Jon finishes onboarding; after that Jon will be primary, Samir secondary. Ava is on standby for infra escalations.

Jon Rivera: noted. I can take primary at +1h. Can you paste the exact commands you used for the cache pin and the autoscale bump?

Maya Patel: sure — commands I ran in the ops shell: 
```
# bump dedicated pool for hosted-api
redwoodctl capacity adjust --pool hosted-api --gpu-count 4 --min 2 --max 8 --reason "latency spike"
# pin cache policy on ded-3
redwoodctl runtime cache set --node ded-3 --policy pinned --ttl 600
```
Also I applied temporary fallback routing in the policy: 
```
route-policy update hosted-api --fallback model-v1-hot --threshold 1200ms
```

Samir Shah: re: INC-4217 — I filed TKT-9842 to track the customer outreach and billing credits assessment. Will add the perf graphs.

Ava Chen: thanks Samir. On KV thrash, did you check if there was a noisy tenant? last time it was a customer job hitting long sequences.

Maya Patel: yes, ded-3 logs showed repeated long prefix requests from tenant "wallet-svc", ~20K requests in 10m. I flagged their traffic to be rate-limited by policy and DM'd the account manager.

incidents-bot: [ALERT] INC-4221 resolved -> severity downgraded to SEV3. Postmortem required if unresolved within 72h.

Jon Rivera: ok taking primary at 1932104834 (in ~1h). Samir you remain secondary until 1932140000. Ava will handle infra pager if incidents escalate out of runtime scope.

Maya Patel: handoff checklist I completed: 
- Updated incident channels and timelines 
- Bumped capacity and pinned cache (commands above) 
- Filed TKT-9842 for customer comms 
- Noted runbook and playbook links in case notes: https://redwood.internal/runbooks/latency-spike and https://redwood.internal/runbooks/cache-thrash

Jon Rivera: I reviewed runbooks — two quick pointers for next oncall: 
1) If latency spike repeats after fallback, escalate to model-ops for a canary rollback: pager: @model-ops and use `redwoodctl model rollback hosted-api model-v1-hot`
2) If KV thrash persists, scale read-only workers first to drain KV pressure, don't restart primary processes until drains complete.

Samir Shah: action items I'm taking: 
- Add histograms to TKT-9842 for top N tenants by request rate (ETA 3h)
- Schedule a post-handoff async sync for 16:00 PT with product + AMs to coordinate customer comms (I'll create calendar invite)

Maya Patel: thanks. One coverage gap to call out: Ava and I noticed no clear backup for region-eu during night shift — only one oncall rotation currently able to access eu VPC. If Jon is in US timezone tomorrow morning we may have a 4hr window with slow response. Recommend we update the on-call rota or add a daytime EU buddy.

Ava Chen: noted. I can be EU fallback for the next 48h while we update rota, but we should create a short runbook item for cross-region credentials and add to the handoff template.

Jon Rivera: I'll add the EU fallback to the rota and create an RFC in #eng-oncall to make the change permanent. Also I'll ping infra for ephemeral bastion creds for the shift.

Maya Patel: final notes before I log off: 
- Pages to watch: hosted-api (latency), embedding-service (queue depth), dedicated pools (cpu steal)
- Retros: I'll draft a blameless postmortem template for INC-4217 and INC-4221 and drop in #postmortems by EOD.

ops-bot: Deployment notice: rolling restart scheduled on ded-3 at 1932106000 for kernel patch (non-blocking). If impacted, update incident.

Samir Shah: follow-ups: 
- Samir -> TKT-9842: add customer contact + proposed credits (done)
- Jon -> update rota + create RFC (in progress)
- Ava -> add cross-region credentials steps to handoff template (in progress)
- Maya -> postmortem drafts (pending)

Jon Rivera: sounds good. I'll take primary handoff at 1932104834 and post an interim status update if anything new within 30m. If not, I'll confirm quiet status. :thumbsup:

Maya Patel: thanks team, leaving notes and Slack thread pinned. ping me if anything breaks while I'm out. :wave:

title: #eng-platform: 1902001234
preview:
Maria Nguyen: kicking off 6h soak in staging on quota/rate pipeline targeting per-key burst windows. synthetic client ramp 0->500 RPS, with short sequences + long tails. initial run shows steady perf, but after ~2h saw ~10% 429s concentrated on a small set of keys. logs and config below.

Samir Patel: can you paste the test config and the throttling policy you used? were we hitting per-org or per-key limits? also did you enable burst windows?

Maria Nguyen: test config:
```yaml
clients: 50
rps_per_client: variable
pattern: mix(short:70%, long:30%)
duration: 6h
target: /v1/generate
auth_keys: rotating 200 keys
throttle_policy: per_key=1000rpm, burst_window=30s, reservoir=2000
```

Jordan Lee: seeing 429s after 2h usually means reservoir burned then regen lag. Did you check quota replenishment logs? link: https://staging.observability/queries/abc123 :chart_with_downwards_trend: 

Ava Chen: telemetry shows regen_rate spikes and then drops -> maybe our leaky bucket refill math wrong when keys are hot. also noticed a correlation with routing spillover to the secondary pool around 02:10 UTC.

Maria Nguyen: added logs:
```
[02:09:58] key=k-147 used=1980 reservoir=20 -> 429
[02:10:05] route=secondary_pool latency=+120ms
```

Riley: quick thought — our fallback to secondary pool is slower and doesn't inherit the same reservoir counters, causing double counting. Could be the enforcement RPC to quota-service timing out under pressure.

Samir Patel: can we recreate with single-key hotkey test (1 key @ 5k RPS) to verify regen vs enforcement RPC latencies? also adjust burst_window larger to 60s and see.

Maria Nguyen: starting a focused 30m hotkey test now. will tag results. @infra-bot please spin an extra quota-service instance in staging for test.

infra-bot: action taken — scaled quota-service from 2->4 instances in staging (request id: qsvc-789). :rocket: 

Jordan Lee: if scaling fixes it, we need default fallback policy to route to pool that shares counters, or make enforcement stateless via token-bucket on edge.

Ava Chen: +1 to edge token-bucket for quick failfast, and long term unify counters. I'll add as RFC item.

Maria Nguyen: results: hotkey 30m: 98% success at 5k RPS but still 2% 429 clustered when route spill occurred. handing off to Samir to dig into enforcement RPC traces. next steps: (1) Samir investigate RPC latencies and timeouts, (2) Ava draft RFC for edge token-bucket, (3) infra consider sticky routing or shared counters. pinging product later for rollout UX changes.

Samir Patel: acked, will post traces in next 2h.

Riley: thanks, nice catch. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-ml: 1898765432
preview:
Ravi Patel: Quick follow-up from the precision-profiles RFC — we need a concrete SDK contract for how clients express precision preferences and what to expect from runtime fallbacks.
Sofia Kim: +1. Customers keep asking 'if I ask for FP8 what guarantees do I get vs INT8/4bit'. we should be explicit.
Ava Chen: Can we map it to a small set of fields? e.g. `precision_preference`, `quality_floor`, `latency_target_ms`, `allow_fallbacks` — then runtime negotiates.
Jamal Ortiz: That seems reasonable. Two edge cases: (1) model incompatibility (2) hot fallback during overload. Need to define semantics.
Sofia Kim: For (1) return an immediate structured error if the model binary doesn't support requested precision, unless `allow_fallbacks=true`. For (2) runtime can switch to a fallback precision but must emit telemetry and a `precision_change` event in the response header.
Ava Chen: Example sketch: ```json
{
  "precision_preference": ["FP8","INT8","INT4"],
  "quality_floor": 0.95,
  "latency_target_ms": 120,
  "allow_fallbacks": true
}
```
Ravi Patel: Nice. We should avoid customers thinking `quality_floor` is absolute — it's a relative acceptance threshold tied to an eval set. Add `quality_basis` reference id.
Maya Singh: Ops concern: if runtime starts falling back under load we'll get noisy alerts. Can we provide a sampling knob `fallback_sampling_rate` and a summary metric per-customer `fallback_rate`?
Jamal Ortiz: Good call. Also need consistent behavior for streaming vs non-streaming calls — partial tokens might be generated at one precision then continue at another. That's messy. I propose: disallow mid-stream precision switches; only allow switch between requests unless the client set `allow_midstream_switch=true` and we warn about artifacts.
Sofia Kim: Agree. Midstream switch opens up token-level discontinuities. Default: no midstream.
Ava Chen: On the SDK ergonomics side, provide helper enums and a validation error that shows the runtime-compatible list for chosen model. E.g., `Model X supports [FP8,INT8]` — SDK surface should validate pre-flight.
Ravi Patel: Rollout plan: (A) SDK + docs change, (B) runtime emits compatibility discovery endpoint `/v1/models/:id/precision-support`, (C) canary runtime behavior that honors `allow_fallbacks=false` strictly for a subset of traffic.
Maya Singh: For SLAs — we already tie latency SLOs to plan. Should we add precision-specific SLOs? Eg. FP8 requests get a different latency/availability target?
Jamal Ortiz: I think precision affects cost more than baseline availability. Better: expose expected perf delta in model metadata (p95_latency_multiplier for FP8/INT8/INT4) so customers can set budget rules.
Sofia Kim: Telemetry events to emit: `precision_requested`, `precision_negotiated`, `precision_fallback_triggered`, `precision_change_reason`, `quality_estimate` (when available).
Ava Chen: UX: SDK should surface a small `NegotiationResult` object with fields: `final_precision`, `fallback_chain`, `quality_estimate`, `warnings`. Keep it simple.
Ravi Patel: Ops-bot note: @ops-bot can we add an alert when `fallback_rate` > 2% for 5m for any customer?
ops-bot: Received. Creating alert rule `precision_fallback_rate_high` scoped to customer namespace. :white_check_mark:
Maya Singh: One more: gating for canary — require perf + quality shadow eval (1000 prompts) before allowing `allow_fallbacks=true` in prod for a model variant.
Jamal Ortiz: Agreed. We'll add a short checklist to the canary playbook.
Sofia Kim: I'll draft the SDK proto and docs blurb, pinging @Ava and @Ravi for review.
Ava Chen: I'll add the `precision_support` discovery endpoint and the `NegotiationResult` shape to the SDK.
Ravi Patel: Great — let's sync in the RFC follow-up tomorrow 10am. Short action items: Sofia: docs + SDK spec draft; Ava: endpoint + schema; Jamal/Maya: ops canary rules & telemetry; I'll update the RFC thread with this decision. :thumbsup:

Ava Chen: Can we map it to a small set of fields? e.g. `precision_preference`, `quality_floor`, `latency_target_ms`, `allow_fallbacks` — then runtime negotiates.
Jamal Ortiz: That seems reasonable. Two edge cases: (1) model incompatibility (2) hot fallback during overload. Need to define semantics.
Sofia Kim: For (1) return an immediate structured error if the model binary doesn't support requested precision, unless `allow_fallbacks=true`. For (2) runtime can switch to a fallback precision but must emit telemetry and a `precision_change` event in the response header.
Ava Chen: Example sketch: ```json
{
  "precision_preference": ["FP8","INT8","INT4"],
  "quality_floor": 0.95,
  "latency_target_ms": 120,
  "allow_fallbacks": true
}
```
Ravi Patel: Nice. We should avoid customers thinking `quality_floor` is absolute — it's a relative acceptance threshold tied to an eval set. Add `quality_basis` reference id.
Maya Singh: Ops concern: if runtime starts falling back under load we'll get noisy alerts. Can we provide a sampling knob `fallback_sampling_rate` and a summary metric per-customer `fallback_rate`?
Jamal Ortiz: Good call. Also need consistent behavior for streaming vs non-streaming calls — partial tokens might be generated at one precision then continue at another. That's messy. I propose: disallow mid-stream precision switches; only allow switch between requests unless the client set `allow_midstream_switch=true` and we warn about artifacts.
Sofia Kim: Agree. Midstream switch opens up token-level discontinuities. Default: no midstream.
Ava Chen: On the SDK ergonomics side, provide helper enums and a validation error that shows the runtime-compatible list for chosen model. E.g., `Model X supports [FP8,INT8]` — SDK surface should validate pre-flight.
Ravi Patel: Rollout plan: (A) SDK + docs change, (B) runtime emits compatibility discovery endpoint `/v1/models/:id/precision-support`, (C) canary runtime behavior that honors `allow_fallbacks=false` strictly for a subset of traffic.
Maya Singh: For SLAs — we already tie latency SLOs to plan. Should we add precision-specific SLOs? Eg. FP8 requests get a different latency/availability target?
Jamal Ortiz: I think precision affects cost more than baseline availability. Better: expose expected perf delta in model metadata (p95_latency_multiplier for FP8/INT8/INT4) so customers can set budget rules.
Sofia Kim: Telemetry events to emit: `precision_requested`, `precision_negotiated`, `precision_fallback_triggered`, `precision_change_reason`, `quality_estimate` (when available).
Ava Chen: UX: SDK should surface a small `NegotiationResult` object with fields: `final_precision`, `fallback_chain`, `quality_estimate`, `warnings`. Keep it simple.
Ravi Patel: Ops-bot note: @ops-bot can we add an alert when `fallback_rate` > 2% for 5m for any customer?
ops-bot: Received. Creating alert rule `precision_fallback_rate_high` scoped to customer namespace. :white_check_mark:
Maya Singh: One more: gating for canary — require perf + quality shadow eval (1000 prompts) before allowing `allow_fallbacks=true` in prod for a model variant.
Jamal Ortiz: Agreed. We'll add a short checklist to the canary playbook.
Sofia Kim: I'll draft the SDK proto and docs blurb, pinging @Ava and @Ravi for review.
Ava Chen: I'll add the `precision_support` discovery endpoint and the `NegotiationResult` shape to the SDK.
Ravi Patel: Great — let's sync in the RFC follow-up tomorrow 10am. Short action items: Sofia: docs + SDK spec draft; Ava: endpoint + schema; Jamal/Maya: ops canary rules & telemetry; I'll update the RFC thread with this decision. :thumbsup:

Maya Patel: Heads up team — GreenMeadow just hit our alert thresholds. Usage down ~48% over the last 30 days, seat activity below SLA, and they swapped procurement lead last week. They’re running a side eval of AtlasAI (heard from AE). Need quick triage and owner assignments.
Diego Ramos: Thanks Maya. Do we have a breakdown by workspace/feature? Is the drop sudden or gradual? cc @Ava for logs.

title: #all-hands: 1842509999
preview:
Noah Kim: Quick Q from the all-hands: can we get a concise definition of patch vs minor vs major for the release calendar?
Zara Hughes: Yup. Short answer: patch = urgent bug/security fix; minor = regular feature/iterative work; major = API or infra contract change or anything that needs cross-team migration.
Maya Singh: Also need clarity on frequency: you mentioned 'cadence' in the talk — is that frozen or guidance?
Ava Chen (CTO): Guidance. Plan is: weekly small patches as needed; a two-week minor cadence (feature train); and a monthly window for bigger platform/major-compat releases. Exceptions allowed with RFC and coordinating owners.
Ben Carter (EngOps): For rollouts we'll use staged expansion with monitoring gates. Suggested default: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100%. Smaller services can skip steps.
Noah Kim: What monitoring gates should be in the PR?
Zara Hughes: Include: baseline error rate, baseline p95 latency, a list of synthetic "golden" prompts for perf and correctness checks, and any route-specific counters. Ben will add a small checklist to the PR template. :white_check_mark:
Oncall-bot: 
```
Default rollout timeline:
- 1% (5m checks)
- 10% (15m checks)
- 50% (30m checks)
- 100% (1h stabilization)
Auto-cut triggers (any one):
- 5xx rate increase > 0.2% absolute for 10m
- request error rate > 3x baseline for 5m
- p95 latency > 1.5x baseline for 15m
- increase in model hallucination signals (eval drift) > threshold
```
Maya Singh: I like the 1% start for risky changes. How involved is oncall at each step?
Ben Carter (EngOps): Oncall watches for alerts and has authority to pause/rollback. They do not need to manually ack each expansion unless an alert fires. Owners must post progress in #release-updates.
Noah Kim: What are the quality gates pre-merge for a minor?
Zara Hughes: Required: passing unit tests, staging smoke, perf regression test against the golden prompt set (no >4% regression in throughput or p95), and model-output regression check for any generation/model-selection changes. E2E tests when routing/model selector is affected.
Ava Chen (CTO): Important: any change affecting model selection, quantization, or tokenization needs a model-eval run and a second reviewer from ML infra.
Maya Singh: Post-release: when do we expect incident writeups?
Zara Hughes: Any customer-impacting regression or rollback => 72h mini postmortem filled in the playbook. Less severe issues should still get a short incident note within a week.
Noah Kim: SLA tie-ins: does the cadence change when error budget is low?
Ava Chen (CTO): Yes. If monthly error budget remaining < 15% we auto-enforce a freeze for non-critical changes until engineering triage clears the budget. EngOps publishes alerts at 50% and again at 15%.
Ben Carter (EngOps): Enforcement: automatic block in release tool when <15% error budget. Exceptions need exec signoff documented in the release ticket.
Maya Singh: Branching policy for hotfixes?
Zara Hughes: Hotfixes: branch from stable (patch/*), include changelog and tests, and get two approvers if touching public APIs. Merge back to main after validation.
Noah Kim: Can Ben drop the PR checklist and a short cheatsheet into #eng-process?
Ben Carter (EngOps): Will do. I'll post a one-pager with the default rollout snippet and PR checklist by EOD tomorrow.
Ava Chen (CTO): Thanks — we'll add the one-pager to the onboarding playbook and link it from the release RFC template.
Noah Kim: Appreciated, this clears up a lot.

title: #all-hands: 1839004567
preview:
Samir Patel: Quick follow-up from today's all-hands — can leadership clarify the new release cadence and what counts as a patch vs minor vs major?
Priya Nair: Great q. TL;DR: patches = hotfix/SEV1 regressions, minor = regular feature releases, major = breaking changes or platform upgrades that need coord with infra.
Lena Ortiz: Also want to know where the rollback/autocut threshold is — we had a canary that spiked p95 last week.
Ava Chen (CTO): High level: aiming for a biweekly feature train (every 2 weeks) for non-blocking improvements, monthly non-breaking platform release window for larger work, and immediate patches for incidents.
Ava Chen (CTO): Patch criteria: any customer-facing correctness or reliability regression (SEV1/SEV2) or security fix -> ship as patch ASAP. Minor = backward-compatible features that pass QA. Major = infra or API contract changes, requires RFC + cross-team signoff.
Diego Ruiz: Can you spell out the canary rollout steps and thresholds?
Priya Nair: Canary plan: 5% -> 25% -> 100% over controlled windows. Metrics to watch: error rate, latency p95, and request drop rate. Auto-rollback triggers if error rate > 3x baseline AND sustained for 5m, OR p95 latency > 2x baseline for 10m.
Riley (Eng Manager): Are those thresholds per-route or global?
Priya Nair: Per-route where possible; if metric collection missing, fallback to service-level. We should add missing route-level meters as tech debt. :memo:
Samir Patel: What's the role of oncall in a rollout? Do they need to ack every canary step?
Morgan Lee: Oncall is primary for rapid rollback decisions. They don't need to ack 5% -> 25% unless they see alarms, but they should be watching the rollout channel and confirm within 10m if an alert fires.
Ava Chen (CTO): We want to avoid micromanaging every step. Oncall has authority to pause/rollback. Release owner is responsible for monitoring and for having a rollback plan in the PR description.
Lena Ortiz: Quality bar check — what minimal tests are required before a minor/feature train lands?
Priya Nair: Required: unit tests, smoke integration test, and a perf regression check against a reference prompt set with a threshold (no >5% regression in p95 token latency). E2E tests if the change touches routing or model selector.
Samir Patel: And post-release — do we require a postmortem for patch/rollback events?
Riley (Eng Manager): Postmortem required for any rollback or customer-impacting incident. Short blameless postmortem template in Confluence should be filled within 72h.
Diego Ruiz: SLA/SLO question — are there updated SLO targets tied to these cadences?
Ava Chen (CTO): SLOs remain: 99.9% availability for hosted API, latency SLOs per model/route documented in Console. Error budget policy: if weekly error budget drops below 20% remaining, we restrict non-essential releases and prioritize stabilization.
Morgan Lee: Who enforces release freezes when error budget is low?
Priya Nair: EngOps will publish a warning when error budget < 50% and apply a hard freeze if < 20% (exceptions require exec signoff).
Samir Patel: Can someone paste the rollout example we should copy into PR templates?
deploy-bot: 
```
Rollout plan:
- Canary 5% (10m monitoring)
- Expand to 25% (20m monitoring)
- Full rollout 100% (30m monitoring)
Rollback criteria:
- error_rate > 3x baseline for 5m
- p95 latency > 2x baseline for 10m
- increase in 5xxs > 0.5% absolute
```
Priya Nair: That snippet is the baseline. Add any service-specific checks under "extra checks" in the PR template.
Lena Ortiz: One last thing — does this change how we do hotfix branch naming / cherry-pick policy?
Riley (Eng Manager): Hotfixes should be branched from stable, tagged as patch/* and include changelog note. Cherry-picks to main happen after validation; small teams may do direct cherry-pick with two approvers.
Samir Patel: Thanks all — this helps. Would be great if EngOps could post a short doc with timelines and checklist.
Priya Nair: On it. I'll draft a short checklist and drop it in #eng-process by EOD tomorrow. :thumbsup:
Ava Chen (CTO): Thanks — we'll align this into the playbook and add to next week's onboarding.
Morgan Lee: :eyes: will review checklist once posted.
Samir Patel: Appreciate it.

title: #eng-platform: 1832109999
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick sync — I sketched a tiny decision tree for when our API should surface routing/fallback reasons to customers vs when we should return a generic tombstone (for console badging + client UX). Link: https://internal.redwood/notes/error-surface-decision-tree :eyes:
Ravi Patel: Nice. One quick question — do we ever expose variant ids (e.g., model-v2-quant) in the tombstone payload? feels risky for docs/contract coupling.
Jordan Kim: Console ask: if we tombstone, can we show a subtle badge on SDK calls like 'served-with-fallback' + tooltip explaining fallback but not exposing variant id? That keeps product teams informed without leaking infra detail.
Leah O'Neill: Telemetry note — if we do this, emit a low-cardinality metric: `client_exposure.fallback` with labels `reason` (cap/burst/fallback), `route` (api-route-name), `fallback=true/false`. Keep variant id out of the metric.
Marco Ruiz: API contract thought: do we prefer returning 2xx with an `x-redwood-surface` header or return 424/5xx? Some customers expect HTTP semantics for retries.
Ava Chen: Good points. My current leaning: return 200 + body + header `x-redwood-fallback: tombstone` for soft fallbacks (non-terminal) so apps can decide. Use 424 for terminal degraded results that we want clients to treat as failures.
Ravi Patel: Agree on headers. For SDKs we can map `x-redwood-fallback` -> SDK event so console can correlate without exposing route ids in UI.
Jordan Kim: UI copy idea for tooltip: "A request was served using a fallback path to preserve availability. Results may vary. See diagnostics in Console (team-only)." Keep customer-facing wording calm.
Leah O'Neill: Also add a trace/span tag `fallback.tombstone=true` so traces can be filtered. Don't add model variant tags to traces either — only use internal-only debug flags.

title: #announcements: 1813456789
preview:
Ava Chen: Morning all — quick executive snapshot for the week. High level: product momentum is strong, we closed two Dedicated deals, and we’re green on the Q2 roadmap. A few priorities and a short ops note below.

Ava Chen: Metrics highlights: MRR +8% WoW, new ARR booked ~$2.4M this week (two mid-enterprise deals), p95 inference latency 140ms, p99 430ms, platform uptime 99.95% last 7 days after mitigation. Cost/unit improvements: Optimize suggestions reduced token cost ~6% on average in pilot.

Ava Chen: Priorities for next week: 1) Finish Dedicated onboarding checklist for Acme & Nova (customer success + infra) 2) Ship RBAC + audit logs in Private GA 3) Push optimized quantization profiles to prod for high-throughput models 4) Roll out updated data retention default (see below).

Ava Chen: Ops note / incident summary — last Tue we had a partial outage impacting some Dedicated tenants during a scale event. Root cause: autoscaler race condition that double-scheduled workers for a small subset of pods, leading to temporary resource pressure and degraded routing. Incident resolved by rolling back the autoscale change and patching the scaler. Postmortem will be out EOD Friday.

Marco Ruiz: Thanks Ava. Quick ask — can we attach the slide deck with the numbers + churn breakout? I’ll share in leadership sync. :white_check_mark:

Priya Nair: From eng side — autoscaler patch landed (PR #4021) and deployed to canary cluster. Patch prevents duplicate scale actions and adds a short lock window. Added a metric to track duplicate-scaling attempts. Need infra to validate on dedicated pools.

Ben Holt: I validated on two dedicated pools. No duplicates observed in the canary run. Scheduling a full smoke this afternoon. We should also add an alert for rapid worker churn > 3 in 1m.

Sofia Park: Heads-up to DevEx & docs teams: we need a short KB and customer-facing blurb about the outage + what we changed. Will draft and post to status.redwood this morning.

Liam O'Connor: Financial impact estimate on the outage: preliminary hit ~ $38k in billable downtime for affected tenants, plus estimated $12k in support hours. Will update after final list of affected customers from CS.

Daniel Kim: On the RBAC + audit GA — what's the rollout cadence? Can we soft-launch to beta customers next week?

Ava Chen: Yes — beta for RBAC + audit logs next Wed (3 days). CS + Solutions will enable for 5 pilot accounts, then 2-week feedback loop before broader GA. Daniel, please own the pilot runbook.

Priya Nair: Action items: 1) Daniel - pilot runbook for RBAC (due Tue) 2) Ben - full smoke test on dedicated pools (today) 3) Sofia - status page copy + customer comms (this AM) 4) Priya - monitor canary and postmortem draft (EOD Fri).

Marco Ruiz: Small policy change to note: default data retention window will change to 30 days for new accounts, with opt-in for longer retention (90/365) under contract. This reduces surface for regulated customers by default and aligns with compliance ask. We’ll include a clear opt-in flow in Console.

Sofia Park: I’ll add the console text and an inline tooltip. Draft for review in #product this afternoon.

Daniel Kim: Noted on ownership. Will have runbook draft by EOD Tue.

Ben Holt: Smoke test passed on canary, no regressions observed. Scheduling broad rollout tonight at 22:00 PT; will monitor autoscaler metrics and rollback if we see duplicate-scaling > 0.

deploy-bot: deploy-bot: Canary deployment: redwood/autoscaler-service@patch-4021 completed — 1813456892 — status: success

Ava Chen: Thanks team — solid sprint. I’ll send a short exec note to folks not in this channel summarizing the GA windows, the policy change, and the postmortem link once it’s ready. Back to deep work. :sparkles:

title: #eng-runtime: 1799954321
preview:
Ava Chen: Ran a focused probe for streaming vs non-streaming using the new kernel-probe flags. Goal: understand kernel selection points, batching heuristics, and KV cache churn under chunked responses. Short summary & raw numbers below, please re-run or comment if you can.

Miguel Torres: Which model/infra/resolution? gpus?

Ava Chen: 2.7B open model on A10G pool (single zone), runtime=release-2026-11. Probe toggled prefer-fused, fallback-matmul, and kernel-switch threshold at token=128. Harness: replay v2 with synthetic client that supports chunking and pacing.

Riley Park: Cool. Did you capture p50/p95/p99 + qps + host cpu?

Miguel Torres: From the primary run (chunk_size=64, no client pacing, warmup 5m): streaming => p50=48ms p95=210ms p99=380ms, qps=1180, host CPU=62%. Non-streaming => p50=65ms p95=320ms p99=560ms, qps=1350, host CPU=44%.

Ava Chen: TL;DR: streaming wins tails but costs CPU and reduces batching efficiency due to client-side backpressure and more frequent kernel switches mid-request.

perf-bot: Perf job 1799954321-rt uploaded: https://ci.internal/runs/1799954321 - includes flamegraph (kernel/cpu), kernel-switch timelines, and KV-cache heatmap. Summary: KV hit-rate dropped ~9% on streaming runs because partial-chunk writes cause earlier segment evictions.

Riley Park: That KV observation matches what I expected. Streaming breaks contiguous prefix access so KV segments get less reuse. Did you try pinning prefixes in the cache?

Miguel Torres: Quick experiment: pin TTL=30s for prefix segments -> streaming p95 improved from 210ms -> 200ms and qps 1180 -> 1220, but memory usage +12%. Pin TTL=120s further improves qps but memory spike unacceptable on small nodes.

Ava Chen: Noted. Batching heuristics: continuous batching (streaming) had avg batch size ~12 requests, non-streaming ~24. So non-streaming packs better which explains the qps advantage.

Riley Park: Client readloop behavior matters too. With chunked responses we saw tight readloop syscall wakeups; that causes CPU sys% to spike and increases context switches. Did you try adding pacing in client loop?

Miguel Torres: Yes. Re-run with chunk_size=128 and client pacing=0.5ms: streaming p95=190ms, qps=1250, host CPU=55% (much better). So pacing reduces syscall storms and improves batching backpressure.

Ava Chen: Great. Also watched kernel selection logs: a bunch of mid-request switches when seq_len crosses 128 boundary due to dynamic selection logic. That increases overhead because we rebind kernels and sometimes fall back to matmul path temporarily.

Riley Park: We should try prefer-fused=true so we bias toward fused-attn earlier. Might reduce mid-request oscillation.

Miguel Torres: Tried prefer-fused=true on a stab: p95 improved ~8-12ms on streaming, kernel-switch events reduced by ~30%. Tradeoff: fused path uses slightly more vram.

Ava Chen: Action items from this thread:
- expose small TTL knob for prefix pinning and test 30/60/120s in matrix
- add client pacing option to replay harness and test 0.25/0.5/1.0ms
- run kernel-probe with prefer-fused toggled and record switch counts
- ensure warmup step (10m) before measuring to get stable KV warm cache

infra-bot: queued replay run: replay-job-1799954321-2 (cmd: replay --id=1799954321 --chunk=128 --pacing=0.5 --pin-ttl=30 --warmup=600). ETA ~45m.

Riley Park: For apples-to-apples we also need same batch targets and same concurrency envelope. Warm cache is key — add explicit warm trace run with identical trace and clients.

Ava Chen: Agreed. I'll update the harness and schedule a nightly matrix: chunk_size=[64,128,256], pacing=[0,0.5], pin_ttl=[0,30]. Also add A100 vs A10G comparison per Miguel's ask.

Miguel Torres: If anyone has spare A100 time, please flag. Curious if kernel selection differs enough to change the prefer-fused recommendation.

Ava Chen: Adding A100 to the run matrix. Thanks all :rocket:

Riley Park: FYI leaving a tiny note here: consider adding a kernel-probe flag to log per-request kernel decision points (lightweight) — saved us debugging time.

Ava Chen: Will add probe-logging opt. Closing after runs complete and I'll post consolidated numbers + graphs in #eng-runtime.

preview:
Ava Chen: Morning team — quick relay before I hand off. I'm primary until 12:30 PT, Samir you're secondary starting 12:00 PT so expect the warm handover around 12:15. Coverage gaps: APAC region has no local secondary during this window, so if we get region-specific pagers ping @ops on-call first.
Samir Patel: noted. I'll be available for escalation and can take primary exactly at 12:30. Planning to check the KV-cache health and node counts at 12:05 to confirm warmstart state.
Lucia Gomez: I can stay on standby for model fallbacks. If a p99 spike happens, please try the model-variant fallback before scaling GPUs.
Ava Chen: Runbooks I recommend pinned for this shift:
- KV cache stall / thrash: https://wiki.redwood.ai/runbooks/kv-cache-stall
- Model fallback + canary rollback: https://wiki.redwood.ai/runbooks/model-fallback
- Region routing & failover: https://wiki.redwood.ai/runbooks/region-failover
Use the 'Shift Handoff' checklist in the console before leaving: Console > Oncall > Shift Handoff.
incident-bot: Pager #INC-2317 — p99 latency spike on hosted cluster us-west-2 (5m avg). Severity: P2. Affected routes: /v1/generate (redwood-large-2). Trigger: token-latency > 1200ms.
Priya Reddy: Confirming—seeing increased inflight and KV cache eviction rate on node group rg-infer-02. CPU fine, GPU memory elevated. Could be prefix cache churn.
Samir Patel: Quick triage steps (do these in order):
1) Check autoscaler status: `kubectl -n inference get hpa,po -l app=inference -o wide`
2) Inspect KV cache hitrate in metrics: Grafana dashboard -> Inference / KV Cache -> last 15m
3) If hitrate < 40% and eviction rate > 200/s, flip to fallback model-variant: `curl -X POST https://controlplane.internal/api/v1/rollouts/fallback -d '{"service":"us-west-2","variant":"quantized-medium"}'`
4) If fallback does not reduce p99 within 2m, scale up dedicated pool: `infractl scale --pool usw2-infer --replicas 3` (ops approval required for > +2 replicas)
Lucia Gomez: If you do fallback, watch quality alerts on Evals -> Live. If regressions > 5% abort and revert.
Ava Chen: If it's a region-only degradation, route traffic to us-east fallback with policy: Console -> Routing -> Temporary Route -> Percentage 30%. Keep ops informed.
ops-bot: Note: scaling requests require a manual approval from SRE leads outside of business hours. Use /approve-scale in the infra channel.
Samir Patel: Quick commands for safe rollbacks:
```
# check active rollout
curl -sS https://controlplane.internal/api/v1/rollouts/active | jq .
# trigger canary rollback
curl -X POST https://controlplane.internal/api/v1/rollouts/rollback -d '{"id":"$(cat /tmp/last-rollout-id)"}'
```
Priya Reddy: Customer impact callout — list of customers hitting us-west: internal `billing-export` shows high QPS from three heavy tenants (acct-441, acct-88, acct-edge). If we escalate to dedicated scaling, flag potential cost overage and notify account teams.
Ava Chen: Communication plan: if p99 > 1.5s for 10m — declare P1 degraded, open incident channel #inc-usw2-latency, call primary pager, and notify sales/CS with the templated message (link: https://wiki.redwood.ai/templates/degraded-service-notice).
Lucia Gomez: Small tip — sometimes prefix cache rebuild helps: touch the warmcache job `kubectl -n infra create job warmcache-$(date +%s) --image=internal/warmcache:latest` only if you trust the job artifacts.
Samir Patel: I'm going to run a quick HPA check and look at eviction rates. If I see sustained eviction I'll trigger fallback and notify.
Ava Chen: Thanks — leaving now. Samir you'll be primary at 12:30. Priya, Lucia on standby. If anyone picks this up before 12:30, just add note here and @ops-bot.
Samir Patel: Taking over early. I'll post a short post-handoff summary in 10m with key metrics.
incident-bot: Pager cleared — latency returned to normal range (median 320ms, p99 780ms). Auto-note: incident closed at 12:18 PT. :white_check_mark:
Ava Chen: Great, thanks team. Closing handoff thread. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-security: 1785004321
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick ping — opened PR #4122 that changes how the on-prem agent fetches secrets and updates auth token rotation. Need security eyes and VPC/network implications checked before we merge.
Samir Patel: Thanks Ava — linking the PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/repo/pull/4122. Will take a look in ~2 hrs. What's the risk window if we delay merge?
Ava Chen: If we wait a day it's fine for staging. Prod rollout is scheduled next Tue; prefer to have approvals before Friday so QA can run the on-prem smoke tests.
Priya Nair: High-level: code touches secret fetcher + adds a new `auth_proxy` config flag that can be set to `local` or `vpc`. Need to ensure no plaintext logging and KMS usage is enforced for `vpc` mode.
Ava Chen: Yep — added a migration: `agent.config` gains `auth_proxy: vpc|local` and the code path for vpc uses `kms_key_id`. Small diff snippet in PR:

```diff
+ if (config.auth_proxy === 'vpc') {
+   const token = await kms.decrypt(config.kms_key_id, wrappedToken);

title: #eng-security: 1779004321
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick question — if we deprovision a user via SCIM (Okta), do we strip email addresses from service logs immediately? We're getting a privacy request and need to know whether logs still contain user emails. cc @Ravi Patel @Maya Ghosh
Ravi Patel: short answer: deprovisioning removes account but logs are retained per retention policy. we don't automatically redact fields in historical logs unless a deletion job runs. :frowning:
Lin Ortega: do we have a list of which pipelines ingest raw usernames/emails? I think api-gateway and embeddings-service still log `user_email` in request metadata right now.
SecurityBot: incoming event: `SCIM: user_deactivated` for user alice@example.com at 2026-02-28T14:12:09Z — no automatic log purge scheduled.
Maya Ghosh: for this specific privacy request can we run a targeted redaction? if yes, what's the process — ticket + legal signoff?
Ava Chen: want to avoid a full retention policy change. a one-off redaction is fine if it's documented and auditable. Ravi — can you confirm ops playbook?
Ravi Patel: ops playbook: open PRIVACY-DELETE ticket, include user id + timestamp range, legal approves -> infra run `redact-logs --user-email alice@example.com --services api-gateway,embeddings-service` (this uses redact job in gcs -> bigquery). note: redact job replaces email with `<redacted>` but preserves trace_id.
Lin Ortega: will that break any linked traces in observability dashboards? we rely on email in some ad-hoc dashboards.
Maya Ghosh: good point. preference is to preserve trace_id and user_id but remove PII in message fields. legal okayed that approach in past cases.
Ava Chen: can someone post the redact job snippet? want to sanity-check before opening ticket.
Ravi Patel: snippet:
```
redact-logs 
  --filter "user_email:alice@example.com" 
  --fields user_email,message 
  --replace "<redacted>"
```
Ravi Patel: the job takes ~15m for two services; we'll snapshot and store an audit log in vault/log-redactions/<ticket-id>. :white_check_mark:
Lin Ortega: is there any automation tied to SCIM deprovision that should trigger the ticket? e.g., new deactivation webhook -> create PRIVACY-DELETE draft?
Maya Ghosh: not yet. we can add a step in the SCIM webhook handler to emit a `privacy_review_required` event when `external_system=okta` and `user_role` in `sensitive_roles`.
Ava Chen: sounds good. two asks: (1) file PRIVACY-DELETE ticket for alice@example.com with the redact job attached, (2) open a small PR to add a `privacy_review_required` webhook event on deprovision. will assign to Ravi + Lin. thanks
Ravi Patel: filing ticket now -> PRIVACY-DELETE-4427. attaching job plan. ping @Maya Ghosh for legal signoff.
Maya Ghosh: approved, + please keep audit trail. :eyes:
SecurityBot: deploy check: PR #3214 created for scim-webhook: add privacy event. CI passed.
Ava Chen: perfect — thanks all. I'll update the requester and mark as high priority.

preview:
incident-bot: PagerDuty alert triggered: PD-INV-18872 - inference-proxy edge 5xx burst (threshold: 5m error rate > 4%). Link: <https://pagerduty.com/incidents/PD-INV-18872>
Ava Chen: acked — I'm oncall, investigating. pinging infra to check autoscaler and edge pool.
Sam Torres: seeing a 3–4 minute spike on router-503s for us-west-2, error-rate jump from 0.3% -> 3.6%. latency p95 unchanged. pulling traces now.
Priya Rao: FYI PD escalated to @ops-escalation at +2min. any customer-facing 5xx counts?
Sam Torres: quick query against prod-logs: most 5xx are from internal probes / synthetic checks hitting a new health endpoint. example log line: `GET /_healthcheck 500 from 10.12.8.9`
Luca Bianchi: hm looks like the synthetic runner hit a bad binary and started spamming probes. I've temporarily added a predicate to drop probe traffic at the proxy: `edge.filter.drop_tag == "synth-check"` — rolling at 3 hosts first.
Ava Chen: good call. please comment when rule is live. I'll keep PD acked and watch.
Luca Bianchi: rule is live on canary (host-12,13,14) — error-rate on those hosts dropped to baseline. rolling to remaining hosts in 60s.
pagerduty-bot: Incident PD-INV-18872 acknowledged by Ava Chen.
Sam Torres: dashboards show overall cluster 5xx returning to normal as roll continues. p95 latency steady. looks like noisy-synthetic causing flapping alerts not real customer impact.
Priya Rao: can we silence PD for the alert group until the synthetic owner rolls a fix? otherwise we keep getting pages.
Ava Chen: yes please. applying soft-silence for the alert rule 10m while we verify.
incident-bot: Soft-silence applied to alert group `edge-5xx-spike` for 10m by Ava Chen.
pagerduty-bot: PagerDuty note: alert auto-resolve window started (no further triggers).
Sam Torres: confirm no 5xx spikes in real user traffic (prod-edge logs filtered by UA not containing "synth"): nothing substantial in last 10m.
Luca Bianchi: created a follow-up ticket: INF-4891 to roll back bad synthetic and add better retires on the runner.
Priya Rao: action item: reduce synthetic cadence to avoid bulk probe bursts. assigned to synthetic infra.
Ava Chen: closing thread — alert auto-resolved, PD incident closed (auto-resolve). No customer impact determined. If it flaps again we'll escalate to oncall rotation and do full postmortem. :thumbsup:
pagerduty-bot: Incident PD-INV-18872 automatically resolved (status: resolved)
incident-bot: Note: this thread logged as a transient flapping page caused by synthetic probe noise. Recommend follow-up with synthetic team and consider adding a filter to the alert to ignore known-probe IPs.
Priya Rao: I'll open a short blameless note and attach graphs. tagging @synth-team.
Sam Torres: TL;DR: transient synthetic probe loop produced bursty 5xx; quick in-line edge filter + soft-silence mitigated; no customer impact; INF-4891 created.
Ava Chen: thanks all — monitoring for 30m then will hand back to normal rotation.

title: #customer-success: 1770011223
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick heads-up — Im pulling together an account-health + budget readout for Atlasicas renewal meeting next Thu. Need: token burn snapshot (30/60/90d), activation funnel impact, and any onboarding blockers that tie to cost. @Priya can you grab the latest spend forecast?

Priya Singh: On it. Ill pull the 30/60/90 windows and a run-rate projection. Do we want a blended forecast (hosted + dedicated) or just hosted?

Ava Chen: Blended please — their usage is Hosted with a Dedicated pilot for embeddings. Also include any spikes from bulk-embed jobs.

Diego Ramos: FYI we flagged a surge last month from their nightly batch. I can add a short note on mitigation: suggested `concurrency cap = 8` + `batching = 512` for embeddings.

Marcus Lee: Onboarding blocker: SSO rollout delayed ingest of ~40% of new seats; that suppressed per-user token discount assumptions. Ill summarize in 1-2 bullets.

Priya Singh: Draft numbers for eyeballs:
```
30d tokens: 48M
60d tokens: 86M
90d tokens: 132M
Current run-rate: ~1.46M tokens/day
Forecast (next 90d baseline): 140-180M tokens
Cost impact: +12-18% vs last quarter
```

Ava Chen: Can you also produce conservative/medium/upside scenarios and call out assumptions (e.g., embed pilot converts 10/25/50% of queries)?

Priya Singh: Will do. Conservative = no conversion; medium = 25%; upside = 50%.

Diego Ramos: Risk: nightly large-context job accidentally ran with 4k token prompts twice last week — ~30% single-day spike. Recommend alert on avg tokens/request > 1.2k and a temporary quota guard.

Ava Chen: Marcus, can you confirm onboarding docs mention model/seq-length best-practices?

Marcus Lee: Not yet. Ill update the checklist and add a link in the readout.

cs-bot: Reminder — QBR cost template updated: https://redwood.company/qbr-templates (use `budget-risk` section).

Priya Singh: Deck plan: 1) current health + flags, 2) spend scenarios + assumptions, 3) mitigations + enablement asks.

Ava Chen: Enablement asks: 1) training on embed batching, 2) walkthrough of quota guard config, 3) one-off credit for spike (TBD). Who schedules the training? @Diego?

Diego Ramos: I can run batching session. Propose Wed morning.

Marcus Lee: Ill own quota-guard walkthrough and update onboarding docs.

Priya Singh: Ill recommend a credit range in the executive summary. Also flagging renewal risk: projected spend variance > 15% could trigger procurement pushback.

Diego Ramos: Testing small throttle in staging today — will paste logs if odd.

Priya Singh: Uploaded draft: https://drive.redwood/atlasica/qbr-draft — comments welcome.

Marcus Lee: Commented on slide 2 re: assumptions.

Diego Ramos: Staging throttle test: latency +5ms, throughput -2%, token-cost down 14% for batch jobs.

Ava Chen: Final exec summary should call out 3 quick wins: throttle/batching config, onboarding doc + SSO timeline, contingency credit ask.

Priya Singh: Final deck due Tue 10am PT.

Ava Chen: Closing thread — will ping for anything else. :wave:

incident-bot: :rotating_light: ALERT: PagerDuty P0 fired for service=redwood-inference-prod, symptom=queue_backlog_high. queue_length=12k, tail_latency_95=2.4s, drop_rate=4.8%. Trigger: autoscaler_pending_nodes > 10m. PD link: https://pagerduty.example/incidents/PD123456

PagerDuty: Incident PD123456 created and routed to eng-oncall. auto-escalation in 3m.

Rohan Patel: acked via PagerDuty. I'm on it — looking at autoscaler metrics now.

Ava Chen: saw spike ~20:03 UTC, likely after a rollout for kvcache change. Queue jump correlated with model-hotstart failures?

Samir Gupta: logs show many `gpu-alloc` requests stuck in Pending due to node provisioning throttles from cloud quota. Also leader autoscaler logs: `scale_decision skipped: insufficient node quota` repeatedly.

Maya (SRE): customer impact: Hosted API 95p latency doubled; some streaming responses failing with timeout. Customer-facing error rate ~5% (mostly small customers on shared pool).

Rohan Patel: quick mitigation plan: 1) trigger manual surge of worker replicas 2) fall back to smaller model variant for non-critical routes 3) evict large batch jobs. Trying manual scale now.

Rohan Patel: ```bash
# manual scale
kubectl -n inference-prod scale deployment/worker-pool --replicas=60
kubectl -n inference-prod annotate deployment/worker-pool redwood/manual-scale="true"
```

OpsRelay: automated surge job kicked off: created 8 ephemeral GPU nodes (p3.xlarge equivalent). ETA ~90s.

Ava Chen: I'm toggling routing policy to prefer dedicated pool for enterprise customers to reduce impact on hosted tier. Will post config snippet.

Ava Chen: ```json
{"routing":{"policy":"prefer_dedicated","overrides":[{"route":"/v1/generate","target":"dedicated-pool-enterprise"}]}}
```

Samir Gupta: dropping non-essential heavy batches: `killjob --selector=slot=batch-heavy --grace=10` — killing 7 jobs.

Maya (SRE): putting note in status page draft: "Investigating increased latency and request failures for text generation. Some customers may see degraded performance."

Rohan Patel: manual scale completed, replicas=60, pods becoming Ready. Watching queue.

incident-bot: autoscaler controller reports: `scale_decision: resumed` at 20:06:12 UTC.

Rohan Patel: queue length decreased from 12k -> 3.2k in 2m after scale and batch kills. Tail latency 95p down to 1.1s. Drop rate -> 1.3%.

PagerDuty: PD123456 acknowledged by Rohan Patel. Escalation canceled.

Ava Chen: routing change applied, enterprise routes now on dedicated. Verified 200s for a sample of responses.

Samir Gupta: follow-up: autoscaler root cause appears to be twofold — cloud provider rate-limit throttled node provisioning (quota bursts) and our kvcache warmup increased burst of cold-start memory pressure. Filed infra ticket INFRA-789.

Maya (SRE): impact summary for postmortem: - Duration: 00:07m from page to stable baseline - Affected: hosted API customers, small fraction (~5%) errors - Mitigation: manual scale + batch kills + routing overrides - Action items: add autoscaler soft-surge reserve, pre-warm kvcache during canary, add cloud quota monitors.

Rohan Patel: handing off long-running follow-ups to Ava (next on-call). I'll stay for 10m to confirm no regression. Ava, can you take the postmortem and runbook updates?

Ava Chen: taking handoff. I'll open postmortem and schedule 1:1 with infra to discuss quota. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

incident-bot: Incident PD123456 marked as resolved by Rohan Patel at 20:10:02 UTC.

Maya (SRE): leaving an ops note: `kvcache: enable gradual warmup flag; autoscaler: implement immediate soft-surge` (refs INFRA-789, REX-402).

Samir Gupta: adding monitoring alert: `cloud.node.provision.throttle` to integrate with triage runbook.

Ava Chen: posting postmortem link when ready. Good job triaging quickly.

title: #eng-ml: 1769991111
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick Q about the generation contract — how should we guarantee behavior when a stop sequence and max_tokens fight each other? Seeing a case where `logprobs` are null when the stop appears to trigger mid-token. Example request I used below:
```json
{
  "model": "redwood/transcoder-v1",
  "prompt": "Summarize: ...",
  "max_tokens": 1,
  "temperature": 0.0,
  "top_p": 1.0,
  "stop": ["

"],
  "logprobs": 5
}
```
Any established contract for what gets returned (text, logprobs, truncated indicator) when stop detection, tokenizer boundaries, and max_tokens interplay? :thinking:

Rafael: short answer: stop checking is done against the decoded candidate string after each token step (we do not match inside a token bytewise). so if a stop spans token boundaries you might emit the last token before we detect the stop. that can make the returned text appear to contain the stop or part of it depending on whether we strip it in postprocess.

Samir Gupta: also important: streaming vs non-streaming can differ. in streaming we push tokens as they arrive; detection may happen a step later and we might not retroactively remove an already-sent chunk. we should decide contract for both sync and streaming. if logprobs are missing, that's a bug unless we intentionally drop probs for truncated streams. can you paste the exact response you got?

Ava Chen: pasted the response (trimmed):
```
{text: "Answer...

", logprobs: null, finish_reason: "stop"}
```
So finish_reason says `stop` but `logprobs` is null. With other runs (no stop) we get logprobs fine. model=redwood/transcoder-v1, dedicated env.

Kai Park: my expectation / proposed contract: we should 1) always include `logprobs` for any tokens actually emitted (streamed or final) unless the user explicitly requested none; 2) if detection happens after emission, server may strip the stop text from `text` but still include the token-level `logprobs` for the tokens that were produced before stripping; 3) if we truncate because of `max_tokens` include `finish_reason: "max_tokens"` and a `truncated: "max_tokens"` metadata flag; if we stop due to stop sequences include `finish_reason: "stop"` and `truncated: "stop"` only if we removed part of an emitted token. this preserves backwards compatibility by keeping `logprobs` always present when requested.

Samir Gupta: +1 to explicit truncated metadata. we should also document how we handle stop matching vs tokenization: state that matching is done on the decoded incremental text. add example edgecases: stop spans tokens, stop equals prefix of next token, stop is empty string (disallow).

Rafael: small implementation note: on some quantized runtimes we lazily compute logprobs for the last partial work unit to save cycles; if a stop arrives mid-batch we might not have computed probs by the time we send the final response — that explains nulls. fix is to compute final token scores synchronously when `logprobs` requested. will run quick repro on dedicated pool.

deploy-bot: CI for branch `fix/logprobs-stop-contract` succeeded (build: #8421) :white_check_mark:

Ava Chen: cool — thanks. Rafael can you run the repro with streaming disabled and enabled, and with `max_tokens` = 1/2/10 and `stop` present vs absent? paste minimal failing case. i'll open a tiny docs PR to spell out the contract above (logprobs must be present for emitted tokens; truncated metadata required).

Rafael: yep — will run matrix and post results here by EOD. assigning to myself. :thumbsup:

Ava Chen: perfect, thanks everyone. we'll converge on explicit contract and include a tiny spec example and unit tests for Dedicated/Hosted differences.

title: #eng-platform: 1768584321
preview:
Ava Chen: Hey — noticed an odd billing spike for acct acct-onboard-42 after a model fallback during a streaming request. Console shows negative provisional credits then a compensating positive adjustment. Anyone seen this pattern?
Ravi Patel: Hmm. Can you paste the trace id and a short log excerpt? Likely related to retry/dedupe in routing.
Ava Chen: trace=5f9d2c3e, request id X-Req-Id: 01F7... Attached logs: ```INFO stream_start req=01F7 client=web model=redwood-quantized-v2 route=fallback_to-fp
WARN toolcall_truncate req=01F7 tool=search trunc_after=1024 reason=timeout
INFO stream_resume req=01F7 route=fp model=redwood-fp-v1 retry=1
INFO billing provisional_charge tokens=1532
INFO billing credit_adjustment tokens=-1532 reason=partial_tool_trunc``` 
Maya Singh: key bit: toolcall_truncate + retry caused two accounting events: provisional charge at stream start then a later credit when we detected truncation. Why did provisional charge get negative adjustment before finalization?
Chris O'Neill: Might be our provisional window closing on stream reconnects. When route switches from quant -> fp we currently re-emit a billing provisional event with a new request token delta but missing dedupe metadata.
Ravi Patel: Are the billing events tied to X-Req-Id or to a per-path session id? We had bug where reconnect creates new session id but same request header, leading downstream aggregator to treat them as separate attempts.
Ava Chen: Agree — logs show two provisional events with different session ids but same X-Req-Id. Console aggregation groups by X-Req-Id though, so we see negative credit applied first then a later positive charge, which looks like a spike.
Maya Singh: Quick ask: can we add the routing-session id into the provisional billing metadata and use it for dedupe? Also ensure toolcall truncation emits finalization before crediting.
Chris O'Neill: Suggested patch: 1) include session_id in BillingEvent 2) emit final billing marker on toolcall truncate path before sending credit 3) change console UI to show provisional vs finalized split.
deploy-bot: deploy request queued to eng-platform/billing-fixes branch.
Ravi Patel: Also add a small reconciliation job that looks for provisional negative adjustments without a following finalized event within 30s and flags them.
Ava Chen: For the dashboard: create an alert when net provisional adjustments exceed 1k tokens in 1m for a customer -> send to #incidents. We had a support ticket earlier that was confusing.
Maya Singh: I'll prototype a trace-to-billing join in telemetry that correlates session_id + toolcall_id + X-Req-Id. Should help debug customer reports.
Chris O'Neill: Can someone own the rollout plan? We should canary the billing metadata change with a dedicated account and manually verify console numbers before wide rollout.
Ava Chen: I'll own canary. Plan: rollback flag, canary to acct-onboard-42 for 1 hour, monitor net token drift, then gradual roll. Adding cherry-pick to changelog.
Ravi Patel: Thanks — PR review incoming. If we get the dedupe metadata in BillingEvent that'll probably fix the negative spike.
Maya Singh: FYI I'll add a unit test for the billing dedupe logic and an integration test for streaming+toolcall truncation.
Ava Chen: Great. marking this resolved for now — will post followups after canary.

title: #all-hands: 1768501234
preview:
Ava Chen: thanks everyone for joining the all-hands — quick follow-up on the roadmap items we touched on. 2 quick asks: can product/eng clarify any hard deps between API GA, Console redesign, and Optimize features?
Maya Patel: short answer — there are a few hard deps but several things can be parallelized. i can list them.
Liam O'Neill: +1 — from eng side we need the new routing infra ready before we push the Console's model-selection UI (otherwise it's just cosmetic).
Sofia Gutierrez: to be explicit: Console redesign MVP depends on the routing infra for model pinning and canary flags. not the entire Dedicated stack.
Raj Singh: routing infra = smart-router + model-variant registry + rollout service. the registry work is biggest blocker (3 sprints).
Ella Park: for UX planning we need a stable API surface for model metadata — ideally a small read-only endpoint. that can land earlier.
Maya Patel: propose breakdown:
- API: GA for basic generation + embeddings (core SDK) — target: end of Q1. depends on swagger + auth work only.
- Console MVP: model-explore + rollout controls — target: mid-Q2. depends on routing infra registry (blocking) and on an approved metadata contract from API.
- Optimize: batching/quantization recommender (beta) — target: late Q2. depends on telemetry pipeline (ingest + eval sets) and the Console's eval viewer (for UX).
Ava Chen: do we have contingency if registry slips?
Raj Singh: fallback is to ship Console with read-only selection and warn that pin/rollout controls are disabled. not ideal but keeps momentum.
Liam O'Neill: engineering note: if registry slips >2 sprints we should ship Console without live rollouts and enable via feature flag when registry is ready.
Sofia Gutierrez: action item for product/design: prepare a fallback mock so we can user-test Console flows without full rollout backend.
Ella Park: will produce mock screens by next Wednesday.
Maya Patel: risks: Optimize depends on reliable eval telemetry. infra is ~1 sprint behind due to elastic ingest work. if that slips, Optimize beta will shift.
Ava Chen: can we mitigate by using a trimmed eval pipeline for the beta?
Raj Singh: yes — we can stand up a lightweight ingest (no retention guarantees) to unblock Optimize beta, but it costs ~0.5 engineer for 3 weeks.
Liam O'Neill: OK to reprioritize a half-sprint of infra to unblock Optimize if product signs off.
Sofia Gutierrez: product signs off on that trade for beta. pls confirm costs in the weekly planning sync.
Maya Patel: summarizing next steps:
1) Raj to give exact ETA on registry by Monday (1768580000 window).
2) Ella to deliver fallback mock screens by Wed.
3) Raj to scope lightweight eval ingest and provide estimate.
4) Liam to prepare Console feature-flag plan to ship without live rollouts if needed.
5) Maya to update the public roadmap page and signal any shifts to sales.
Ava Chen: thank you — leaders, please post status in this thread after your Monday planning.
all-hands-bot: meeting notes saved to the company drive: https://docs.internal/redwood/all-hands/1768501234-notes
Maya Patel: noted. i'll update the roadmap PR and ping sales + success channels. :thumbsup:
Liam O'Neill: bump if i don't post engineering plan by EOD Monday.
Raj Singh: will post ETA + scope then.
Sofia Gutierrez: thanks everyone — this helps prioritize user testing and release comms.
Ava Chen: <3

title: #sales: 1765439999
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads-up: FinEdge wants an EU-only POC w/ BYO KMS and APAC only as a disaster recovery target (no active routing). They also want a clear list of subprocessors and an egress diagram for legal. Can presales assemble a quick response + ballpark price delta by EOD?

Luca Romano: Quick Q: Which BYO KMS flavors? They mentioned 'customer-managed keys' but didn't specify provider. We can support AWS KMS in hosted via envelope encryption; Dedicated/Private supports direct customer KMS calls (AWS/GCP/Azure) or HSMs. Need to confirm.

Priya Singh: For hosted with BYO the model is: customer wraps a DEK and uploads, we store wrapped key + attest. If they require direct KMS API calls from compute nodes, that's Private/Dedicated only. I'll add this to the reply.

Marta Kovac: If they absolutely forbid any non-EU subprocessors, note that our control plane currently touches a US analytics pipeline for hosted telemetry. We can offer Dedicated with EU-only subprocessors but it adds provisioning time and cost.

Luca Romano: Cost note: Tenant pinning + reserved capacity in eu-west-1 typically adds ~20-30% to the hosted run-rate for small POCs; BYO-KMS ops + attestation adds a fixed onboarding fee (one-time) ~= $1k-$3k depending on risk review. I'll scope more precise numbers if we have expected qps.

Ava Chen: Can someone draft an ops-friendly routing policy snippet and a short list of subprocessors we can share? Legal will want specifics. Also need to confirm whether APAC cold DR will ever touch control plane.

infra-bot: Automated note: current subprocessor inventory (prod) includes: aws-eu-west-1, aws-eu-central-1, redwood-monitoring-eu, redwood-cdn-global. Hosted telemetry pipeline may use us-east-1 for aggregated analytics unless opt-out is configured. See /ops/subprocessors for full list.

Priya Singh: Draft routing policy (example):
```
tenant: finedge-poc
pin_region: eu-west-1
compute_failover: apac-cold (standby, no state replication)
subprocessors_allowed: [aws-eu-west-1, aws-eu-central-1, redwood-cdn-eu]

title: #eng-platform: 1761123456
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick sync — infra needs a concrete list of KV / prefix-cache signals for the Console dashboards and routing policy UX. Can runtime teams confirm what we can realistically emit today?

Ravi Patel: We can emit hit/miss counters, eviction counters, and a reuse counter scoped to (tenant, model, route). Need to agree on label cardinality.

Juno Park: Agree on labels. Proposal: tenant_hash, model, route, seq_len_bucket (0-128,129-512,>512), workload_tag. Keep tenant hashed to avoid PII.

Eli Morales: For prefix cache specifically we also have prefix_reuse_count and prefix_reuse_window_ms — useful to surface reuse vs short-lived prefixes.

Ava Chen: Good. Console product asks for hit_rate and eviction_rate (ratio) and a per-workload "reuse by workload" bar/heatmap. Also a small table of top N tenants by eviction spikes. Privacy ok with hashed tenant?

Fran López: hashed tenant_id is fine. For top-N, we should sample or only show tenants with consent if enterprise opt-in. We can show anonymized IDs like t_1, t_2 for public orgs.

Ravi Patel: Implementation notes: metrics names and types (prometheus-style) below. We should add a "cache_variant" label (kv/prefix) so dashboards can share panels.

Ravi Patel (code):
```
kvcache_hits_total{tenant_hash="...", model="gpt-open-1", route="/v1/complete", cache_variant="kv", seq_len_bucket="0-128", workload_tag="chat"}
kvcache_misses_total{...}
kvcache_evictions_total{...}
prefix_cache_reuse_total{...}
prefix_cache_reuse_window_ms_sum{...}
```

Juno Park: Also emit kvcache_kv_warmth gauge (0..1) computed as recent_hit_count / request_count so we avoid heavy cardinality on histograms.

Eli Morales: For evictions, we need eviction_reason label (capacity, ttl, manual) to help SRE debug. Also include host_pool and region for Dedicated routing analysis.

Ava Chen: UX ask: Console wants 1) overall hit_rate time series, 2) eviction-rate stacked by reason, 3) reuse-by-workload heatmap, 4) top-tenant anomalies alert (threshold + sudden drop in hit_rate). Are these metrics sufficient?

Fran López: Sounds sufficient. Need retention plan: keep 1s or 10s resolution metrics for 7d, rollup to 1m for 90d. Also add sampling guidance for high-cardinality routes.

Ravi Patel: One more: prefix length bucket label (prefix_len_bucket= "1-8","9-32","33+" ) — reuse varies a lot by prefix length.

ConsoleApp: @Ava we can create dashboard skeleton once metrics emitted. Will need queries for: hit_rate = rate(kvcache_hits_total[1m]) / rate(kvcache_hits_total[1m] + kvcache_misses_total[1m]) and eviction_rate = rate(kvcache_evictions_total[5m]) / rate(kvcache_hits_total[5m] + kvcache_misses_total[5m]).

Ava Chen: Nice. Who owns the ingest + schema? I can own Console mapping, but need runtime/infra to define metric names/labels and agree SLA for delays (e.g., <60s ingestion).

Ravi Patel: Runtime will own emitting and docs. Infra will own pipeline ingestion and schema enforcement. We should file a short RFC for metric names + examples.

Eli Morales: I'll draft a tiny RFC with examples and perf cost estimates for 99th-percentile extra labels. Expecting slight CPU/net overhead; will provide numbers.

Fran López: I'll add retention and access rules to the RFC (anonymization, sampling, top-N policy).

InfraBot: New ticket created: INFRA-3742 "kvcache/prefix metrics ingestion pipeline" — https://redwood.atlassian.com/browse/INFRA-3742 :eyes:

Juno Park: For rollout: let's emit metrics behind a flag (kvcache_metrics_v2) for 2 weeks, then enable by tenant opt-in, then default on. That minimizes noise and gives us rollout signal.

Ava Chen: Timeline: RFC this week, instrumentation PR next week, pipeline ingest and dashboard mock in 2 weeks, canary enable + monitor in week 4. Anyone object?

Fran López: No objections. Please include example queries for the canary tenants in the RFC.

Ravi Patel: I'll add example PromQL and a small exporter patch.

Eli Morales: I'll include cost estimate and a small perf test plan.

Ava Chen: Thanks team. Follow-ups: Eli will post RFC link, Ravi will post metric name PR, Fran will post retention/access doc, ConsoleApp will create dashboard skeleton issue. :thumbsup:

InfraBot: INFRA-3742 updated: linked RFC INFRA-3743, added assignees: Eli Morales, Ravi Patel, Fran López.

Ava Chen: Great, locking. We'll reconvene in the eng-platform weekly sync if anything blocks. :calendar:

title: #product: 1747532100
preview:
Ava Chen: quick design ping — new autoscale mock with a few novel bits: 'min floor' concept, a separate 'burst cap' slider, and a hold-to-apply on the SLO slider to reduce accidental high commits. want copy + accessibility eyes.
Noah Kim: Interesting. "Min floor" language could be confusing with existing "min" field. Maybe show it as "Min (guaranteed baseline)" and keep shorthand label.
Priya Shah: UX concern: hold-to-apply might be annoying for power users. Maybe show a confirmation pill only if change > 20% from current value.
Sofi Gomez: a11y: NVDA reads the new "burst cap" slider as just "slider" unless we add aria-label and aria-valuetext. Also ensure the hold-to-apply action is keyboard activatable (Enter after focus) and that assistive tech hears the pending state.
Marco Rivera: Copy idea for burst helper: "Burst cap: maximum temporary GPUs allowed during spikes. Not permanent; billing only for minutes used." Could also include an info link.
Ellie Tran: Fallback messaging needs to call out routing AND perf impact. Proposed toast: 
```
Fallback engaged — Selected pool exhausted. Traffic routed to Redwood Hosted to maintain availability. Expect higher tail latency; data stays encrypted. Reserve Dedicated capacity to prevent routing. (Learn more)
```
Ava Chen: Good. Also thinking of adding a tiny inline badge near min that reads "min floor active" when min > 0 and burst is off, with tooltip: "Guaranteed baseline capacity to avoid cold starts".
Noah Kim: Visual suggestion: show a faded overlay on the slider track for the burst cap zone so it's clear which part of the slider only applies to short bursts.
Priya Shah: For copy concision: "Burst cap: temporary extra GPUs. Auto-removes after spike." Or even shorter for tiny helper: "Temp extra GPUs — auto-remove."
Sofi Gomez: Keyboard flows: when opening pool selector, focus should move into the dialog and trap; closing returns focus. Also add an aria-live polite region for the cost delta when slider changes so screen reader users hear estimated impact.
Marco Rivera: Cost delta phrasing: show compact: "Est. +$0.12/hr" and detailed hover: "Estimated $0.12/hr based on current traffic profile. Methodology: average token throughput * model unit cost."
Ellie Tran: UX edge-case: if user pins a specific pool but also enables fallback, show a small warning: "Pinned pool may route off-cluster when capacity low." Maybe a toggle to disable fallback.
Ava Chen: Nice — we should surface a 'disable fallback' switch but warn about availability impact. Also add an undo toast for accidental disabling.
ui-bot: note: autoscale-experiment-2024 preview deployed to staging at 2024-12-03T21:10Z. Please test hold-to-apply, nvda, and cost live region.
Noah Kim: Action items: Priya will run two microcopy lengths for burst + min floor; Marco will mock overlay for burst cap and cost delta compact; Sofi will run NVDA + keyboard pass and add aria snippets; Ellie will draft fallback disable flow and undo toast. Target: staged QA by end of week.
Priya Shah: Will post microcopy variants later today.
Marco Rivera: I'll add the faded overlay and the compact est. cost next.
Sofi Gomez: I'll start NVDA testing tomorrow morning and drop exact aria attributes in Figma.
Ellie Tran: I'll write the fallback disable confirmation and undo copy.
Ava Chen: thanks all — will update figma with the hold-to-apply prototype and share the link here when ready. :rocket:

preview:
Ava Chen: :wave: Heads-up — onboarding intake for ~28 trial tenants in EMEA experienced delayed provisioning during yesterday's inference outage. Several have kickoff calls this week and a subset are in the 30-day renewal window. We need coordinated comms, renewal risk flags, and localized enablement. Proposed immediate actions:
1) send a short status + mitigation note to affected tenants with a 2-week credit offer
2) schedule EMEA-friendly office hours for walkthroughs
3) tag renewals for AM outreach. @Miguel Torres @Priya Desai @Riley Patel please confirm.

Miguel Torres: :warning: SRE update — root cause was a KV cache invalidation triggered during an autoscaler bump. Services restored at 02:18 UTC. We'll have a full RCA by EOD.
